Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set up a schedule at the beginning, but between my extracurriculars and everything else going on, it fell apart quickly. I'm someone who learns best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often spend time working through problems physically or making models, but that meant my actual scheduled study time was all over the place. I'd have these bursts of intense studying followed by days where I couldn't bring myself to open anything.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how I was feeling emotionally. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, studying a bit each day, but then anxiety would hit and I'd avoid everything for days. When assignments were due, I'd cram everything into the last possible hours. It wasn't healthy, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle no matter how hard I tried to plan ahead.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or seeing a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my attendance was so spotty. The gamified elements they added actually helped motivate me sometimes—seeing points or progress bars made me want to engage more than just a plain list of tasks would have.
Q4: There were a few weeks where logging in became more routine, especially when they introduced those gamified tasks. Earning badges or seeing my progress compared to goals gave me small dopamine hits that kept me coming back. But it never fully stuck as a habit because life would get overwhelming and I'd fall off again. The routine was fragile and easily disrupted by stress or competing responsibilities.
Q5: The interactive activities and practice exercises were most useful for me because I'm a kinesthetic learner—I need to do things to understand them. Discussion boards were also helpful when I actually participated because explaining concepts to others helped solidify my understanding. The gamified tasks were surprisingly engaging because they broke things into smaller, less intimidating pieces.
Q6: I often ignored the longer reading materials and some of the video lectures, especially the ones over thirty minutes. When I'm anxious, sitting still and passively absorbing information feels impossible. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came. The resources that required sustained attention without any interactive component just felt like too much when I was already overwhelmed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or progress—like completing a practice problem and getting feedback right away. The gamified elements really helped with this because there was visible proof I'd accomplished something. If something felt abstract or like it wouldn't help me pass, my anxious brain would categorize it as optional and push it aside.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping up with required ones. There was always this guilt around them—I knew they'd probably help, but my brain prioritized survival mode. Required activities had the weight of consequences behind them, which ironically made them feel more manageable because the stakes were clear. Optional stuff just added to my mental load of things I 'should' be doing.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials to my tablet so I could study during commutes or when I didn't have internet. I also made physical flashcards and diagrams because writing things out helps me remember better than just reading. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper for hours, which wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. My best learning often happened away from screens.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out lecture slides and annotate them extensively while sitting in a café, working through concepts by hand. I'd also meet with a couple of classmates occasionally to work through difficult material together in person. None of that showed up in the LMS. Some of my most productive study sessions happened completely offline when I needed to escape the anxiety of seeing all my incomplete tasks online.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download PDFs or save videos to watch later offline, especially when I knew I'd have time during transit or at my part-time job during breaks. Once I had the material, I might spend days working through it without ever logging back in. The LMS would show one download, but that single download might represent hours of actual study time spread across several days.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became nearly impossible. When my anxiety spiked, even opening the LMS felt overwhelming because I'd see all the things I'd fallen behind on. Mid-semester was particularly brutal—I had deadlines from multiple courses colliding with personal stuff, and I just shut down. The sadness would creep in and make everything feel pointless, even though I knew I needed to push through.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. The workload across three online courses was intense, and when I got confused about concepts, I'd sometimes spiral into avoidance rather than seeking help. Fatigue from trying to balance everything made it hard to focus even when I did log in. I'd find myself staring at the screen, technically 'using' the LMS but not actually absorbing anything.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to everything I was stressed about. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. There were nights I'd be logged in for hours but accomplish almost nothing because I was too anxious to actually focus. The activity was there, but the engagement wasn't.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially assignments that felt too big or complex. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd look at an assignment, feel overwhelmed by not knowing where to begin, and close the tab. I knew they were important, but my brain would convince me I'd do it better 'tomorrow' when I felt more capable. Tomorrow would come and the cycle would repeat until panic finally forced action.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to actually submit work. My LMS usage would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could override my anxiety and avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less, as sad as that sounds. They were stressful but necessary for me.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more, but negative or critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I craved validation but feared criticism. When feedback was constructive and kind, it helped a lot. When it felt harsh, it reinforced my fears that I wasn't good enough.
Q18: The course structure was a double-edged sword. Having things organized into modules helped me understand what needed to be done, but the sheer volume was intimidating. The gamified elements added later in the course actually helped break things into smaller, more manageable pieces. But when the structure was too rigid or didn't account for students who were struggling, it made me feel even more behind and hopeless.
Q19: The gamified tasks genuinely made engagement easier—having clear, small goals with visible rewards helped my overwhelmed brain cope. What made it harder was when there were too many resources without clear prioritization. I'd get lost trying to figure out what was essential versus supplementary. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like chores rather than genuine learning opportunities.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. High activity can mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to produce quality work, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Sometimes you're researching, reading, re-reading, trying to understand—all that shows as activity but doesn't translate to submitted assignments. It's the gap between effort and output when you're overwhelmed.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people are just more efficient, or they do most of their learning offline through textbooks or study groups. If someone has strong background knowledge or good study skills, they might not need to spend as much time in the LMS. I wish I could be more like that, honestly. My high activity often reflected anxiety and inefficiency rather than productive engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, how long they spend, what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of that time. They can't tell if someone is actually learning or just going through motions while panicking. They miss offline study, emotional states, life circumstances, and whether someone is truly understanding material. Numbers don't capture the human experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, wanting to understand deeper rather than just pass. It's feeling like the learning matters to me personally. Unfortunately, this semester I often felt like I was just surviving rather than truly engaging, even when my activity numbers looked okay.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how many times I sat staring at the screen feeling paralyzed by anxiety. It wouldn't show the offline studying, the tears of frustration, or the genuine effort behind my mediocre submissions. It wouldn't capture that I actually cared deeply about learning but was fighting against my own mental health just to show up. The data shows what I did, not who I am or what I was going through.
